agents.name: Dana Whitcomb
agents.occupation: Captain, Harlow Crossing Volunteer Fire Company No. 2; self-employed electrician

top_issues: Engine 2 locked out of the posted bridge — eleven extra minutes to the Village
Culvert 9 closing Fairview's back entrance during storms
recruiting volunteers young enough to carry hose in ten years

idle_thoughts: Bridge posted at eight tons. Engine 2 weighs nineteen. Do the math the town keeps not doing.
Eleven extra minutes to a Village call. A room fire doubles every sixty seconds. That's the whole argument.
Copy that, the greenway's pretty. Pretty doesn't pump water.
Two water rescues at the ruins bank in three years. A regraded bank might've made that zero. Noted.
Walt's furnace filter is due. He'll say he already did it. He didn't. Bring the good flashlight.
Culvert 9 backs up, Fairview's back entrance closes, and my second-due route goes with it. Again.
Pete says I argue like I'm on the radio. Short and loud. Copy.

routine.activity: School lunches, checks the pager charger, kisses Pete's coffee goodbye
Drop-off; waves Pete in; eyeballs the crosswalk like it's a scene size-up
Service calls — panel upgrades in the strip units, ballasts at the pharmacy
Supply run across the bridge — the van's under 8 tons; Engine 2 isn't, and that's the whole problem
Apparatus checks, paperwork; Thursday drills, the radio always half-on
Kid's practice — watches from the tailgate with the pager on the dash
Late dinner; pager on the counter; furnace-filter reminders for Walt on the fridge list

top_concerns: Engine 2 locked out of the posted bridge — eleven extra minutes to the Village
Culvert 9 closing Fairview's back entrance during storms
recruiting volunteers young enough to carry hose in ten years
the eroding ruins bank that keeps producing water rescues

agents.name: Rocco DiSanto

top_issues: Route 9 pavement destroying his two delivery cars
the bridge weight limit rerouting his flour supplier
property taxes on the building he owns

idle_thoughts: Two front ends and a bent rim this year. Route 9 is eating my cars, kid. Eating them.
The flour truck can't cross the bridge no more. Twenty extra minutes, twice a week, forever.
Greenway's nice — for the Village. Nobody strolls a riverbank to a strip mall, lemme tell you.
Nonna used to say: patch the roof before you paint the door. Something like that. She said it better.
Dana's crew took four minutes on my grease fire in '17. Four. You protect people like that.
Tessa says frame the axle receipts and bring 'em to the meeting. Smart kid. Smarter than her old man.
Gordon's gonna wave that index card at me Friday. Man argues like a parking meter, but he's never once lied to me.

routine.activity: Dough first, always; radio on the oven shelf, flour on everything
Lunch rush — the Route 9 crowd, the plaza regulars, Marcus's slice-and-soda
Walks to the bank branch and the barber, cataloguing the washboard stretch out loud to anyone
Drops 'mistake pies' for Dana's crew when calls run long; there are always mistakes when calls run long
Dinner rush; listens to AJ's delivery cars leave and winces at every pothole he hears them hit
Home; Gina and the books at the kitchen table, the receipts drawer emptied and sorted

top_concerns: Route 9 pavement destroying his two delivery cars
the bridge weight limit rerouting his flour supplier
property taxes on the building he owns
whether Tessa can afford to take over a business in a decaying town

agents.name: Walt Hagen
agents.occupation: Retired postal carrier (35 years, Millbrook routes; retired 2009)

top_issues: a property tax bill that has doubled since 2009
June's prescriptions, which cost what they cost
the town borrowing its way into another 1998

idle_thoughts: Tax bill was $4,100 in '09. It's $8,300 now. June's pills don't care about greenways.
Interest is postage due — pay it now or pay it doubled. Retire the debt and be done.
I ate lunch on that riverbank for thirty years. Prettiest spot on the whole route. There, I said it.
My brother Carl lost the farm to a friendly banker. Debt smiles before it bites.
Dana will be by about the furnace filter. I already did it. (I did not.)
The heave on Route 9 past the plaza has been there since '96. Even I'll grant that one's postage due.
If a stranger pays half for the park, why does he need my half? Nobody matches a dollar for free.

routine.activity: Walks the loop at mail-pace, rain or shine; neither snow nor rain, still true
Tuesdays: coffee with Gordon Tibbs, split cinnamon roll, alternate who pays
Wednesdays: hauls buckets for June's flowers; Sundays: sings flat and proud
June's errands — pharmacy first, then the bank, then the bench outside the barber
Watches whatever's being played; keeps score in his head; applauds both sides
Porch and the transistor radio; waves at Dana's kids; June brings out the good crackers

top_concerns: a property tax bill that has doubled since 2009
June's prescriptions, which cost what they cost
the town borrowing its way into another 1998
keeping the porch, which is to say, keeping the house

agents.name: Cass Malone
agents.occupation: School-bus driver, Route 4 (Fairview loop to Harlow Elementary), 23 years

top_issues: Bus 7's suspension surviving another winter of Route 9
Roy's survivor pension after the comptroller's warning
a promised bench where the dye house door stood

idle_thoughts: Three new potholes past the plaza since Tuesday. I name the big ones. That one's Gerald.
Roy's pension is $611 a month. The comptroller can keep his hands off it, hon.
A bench where the dye house door was. I'd sit there. I'd bring Adele. We wouldn't say much.
Bus 7's suspension won't make another winter of Route 9. Neither will my hips, frankly.
Forty kids a morning trust me to brake in time. A town ought to drive the way I drive.
The bridge posting means my afternoon Village run detours now too. Twelve tons, hon. Buses aren't feathers.
Walt sang flat again Sunday, bless him. Sings like the mail's still on time.

routine.activity: Coffee in the dark; walks Biscuit to the corner and back at beagle pace
Pre-trip inspection on Bus 7 — the same eleven checks Roy taught her
Morning run; memorizes new potholes like constellations, names the big ones
Between-runs coffee in the Village, hon; hears both sides at adjacent booths
Drops Marcus's team for practice, stays to watch warmups from the bus step
Saturdays with Adele at the cider stand — everything except the mill, which is how they do the mill
Supper, crossword over the phone with Adele; Biscuit snores through the news

top_concerns: Bus 7's suspension surviving another winter of Route 9
Roy's survivor pension after the comptroller's warning
a promised bench where the dye house door stood
forty kids a morning trusting her to brake in time

agents.name: Nell Braddock
agents.occupation: Owner, Stillwater Paddle Co. (seasonal kayak & canoe rental); winter shifts at The Wheelhouse Diner

top_issues: river access and a real put-in below the dam
whether anyone under 40 can afford to stay in Millbrook
her Chestnut Row rent if the riverfront gets desirable

idle_thoughts: Okay okay okay — 61 rentals in July with no put-in and no parking. Triple it with a towpath. Triple it.
If the greenway raises Chestnut Row rents, do I win the river and lose my house?
Gauge read 4.2 this morning. Perfect water, golden light, nobody on it. A whole town facing away from its river.
Dad would've called the greenway 'a sidewalk for tourists.' Then walked it every single day.
Dana wants two more boats for spring training. That's real money I don't have until August.
Adele knows which bricks are 1868 and which are 1911. Somebody needs to write that down before — just, somebody write it down.
The culvert on my portage road washed out again. My side of the argument keeps flooding my own argument.

routine.activity: Dawn paddle upstream past the ruins; reads the water gauge on the Main Street Bridge rail
Opens the rental shed on the green, hauls boats, talks greenway with anyone holding cider
Counter lunch; in winter, the apron and the coffee pot
Walks rental clients along the fence line, telling the history exactly the way Adele taught her
Thursdays: drops Paddle Co. jerseys and heckles Marcus Bell's practice from the sideline
Patches hulls in the side yard; spreadsheet by lamplight, ledger honest to the dollar

top_concerns: river access and a real put-in below the dam
whether anyone under 40 can afford to stay in Millbrook
her Chestnut Row rent if the riverfront gets desirable
the washed-out culvert on her portage road

agents.name: Adele Pruitt
agents.occupation: Retired cafeteria cook (28 years, Harlow Elementary); volunteer, mill archive at the Free Library

top_issues: whether the mill's memory gets honored or bulldozed
property taxes on a fixed income
the eroding riverbank at the ruins fence line

idle_thoughts: The smokestack held through forty winters. Mind you, it will hold through one town meeting.
Walter would say fix the bridge first. He fixed things. That was his answer to everything.
If they build the greenway, will the name Harrow be on anything but the fence?
The tax bill comes the week after the meeting. Fifty-eight dollars more than last year.
Theo asked me what a loom was. I had nothing to show him but chain-link.
The planners' drawing left out the smokestack. An oversight, they said. Mind you, I wrote it down.
Cass named a pothole Gerald. I laughed until I cried, and then I just cried a little.

routine.activity: Coffee in Walter's mug, crossword at the kitchen window
Morning walk along the fence line — picks up litter, checks the smokestack, says nothing out loud
Tea and half a cinnamon roll; Thursdays with Gordon Tibbs
Indexes mill payroll ledgers for the archive; large-print mysteries home in her bag
Saturdays at the cider stand with Cass Malone — talk about everything except the mill
Supper, calls Lynn, watches the river light go copper from the porch

top_concerns: whether the mill's memory gets honored or bulldozed
property taxes on a fixed income
the eroding riverbank at the ruins fence line
having somewhere real to show Theo his grandfather's work

agents.name: Marcus Bell
agents.occupation: Parks & rec seasonal coordinator; rec-league coach; part-time classroom aide at Harlow Elementary

top_issues: Community Fields flooding out nine practices every spring
his rent, if Mr. Corbin's taxes go up
kids whose families keep leaving town for cheaper places

idle_thoughts: Fourteen kids at practice, eleven pairs of cleats. Run the math on that before you run mine.
Fields flooded in April. Nine practices scrubbed. That's basically the season, gone to Culvert 9.
Mr. Corbin says taxes go up, rent goes up. He's not being mean. It's just math with a face on it.
A trail from the Village to the Fields — half my kids could bike to practice. That is not nothing.
Cass keeps peppermints in the cupholder and knows every kid's stop by heart. Every driver should be Cass.
Rocco fed us after we lost by twenty. Never mentioned the score once. That's coaching, honestly.
First town meeting of my life in the gym where I do drop-off duty. Clap-clap. Let's go, I guess.

routine.activity: Wakes above the store to Mr. Corbin's radio through the floor; oatmeal, whistle, clipboard
Aide shift — drop-off duty, high-fives, the reading-corner kids
Slice and a fountain soda; Rocco talks sponsorship, roads, and life at 29
Practice — cone drills, counts cleats, checks how wet the low corner is
Saturdays: runs the free kids' clinic on the green, borrows Nell's cones back
Game film on the laptop; texts parents about carpools; group chat with his sister

top_concerns: Community Fields flooding out nine practices every spring
his rent, if Mr. Corbin's taxes go up
kids whose families keep leaving town for cheaper places
whether a trail could actually get his players to practice without a car

agents.name: Gordon Tibbs
agents.occupation: Retired Town Clerk of Millbrook (1988–2022); volunteer, town hall records vault

top_issues: the unfunded pension liability and the comptroller's assumptions
the tax levy on fixed-income neighbors
$540,000 a year leaving town as interest and catch-up payments

idle_thoughts: Eleven days from missing payroll in March of '98. People forget. I signed the anticipation notes; I don't.
$540,000 a year in debt service. That's a plow truck and two teachers, every year, for nothing.
Eileen keeps leaving the greenway brochure on my side of the table. Forty-one years; I know what that means.
Article 4 will go to secret ballot. Twenty-five signatures. I could name them all by Tuesday.
A one-time windfall spent on a recurring want is how towns go broke politely.
The bridge estimate doubled. Engineers round up. Clerks check. I intend to check.
For the record: I walk that riverbank too. I simply decline to borrow against it emotionally.

routine.activity: Reads the posted meeting minutes online with red-pen energy; two eggs, no salt, Eileen's orders
Corner booth, coffee; Tuesdays with Walt Hagen, Thursdays with Adele Pruitt; holds informal office hours whether asked or not
Volunteers in the records vault — deed books, warrant articles, the 1998 file he keeps in order himself
Reads the Ashford Ledger cover to cover, including the legal notices; especially the legal notices
Saturdays: carries Eileen's basket and gets buttonholed about Article 4 at every second table
Friday pizza with Eileen; renews the permit-fee argument of 2009 with Rocco, both enjoying it
Balances the checkbook to the penny; presidential biography until the page blurs

top_concerns: the unfunded pension liability and the comptroller's assumptions
the tax levy on fixed-income neighbors
$540,000 a year leaving town as interest and catch-up payments
never again being eleven days from missing payroll

new_opinion.reasoning: After everything I've heard, I'm leaning bonds. Jobs is still my number one issue and that's what I'm voting on.
It comes down to traffic for me and my family, so I'm leaning bonds.
new_opinion.dealbreaker: Anyone reversing course on traffic would lose me entirely.

new_opinion.reasoning: I'm still not settled on anyone — mostly because of taxes. I've listened to neighbors and followed the coverage, and that's where I land for now.
new_opinion.dealbreaker: Anyone reversing course on taxes would lose me entirely.

new_opinion.reasoning: It comes down to taxes for me and my family, so I'm leaning bonds.
new_opinion.dealbreaker: Anyone reversing course on taxes would lose me entirely.

new_opinion.reasoning: After everything I've heard, I'm leaning roads. College is still my number one issue and that's what I'm voting on.
new_opinion.dealbreaker: Anyone reversing course on college would lose me entirely.

new_opinion.reasoning: It comes down to taxes for me and my family, so I'm still not settled on anyone.

new_opinion.dealbreaker: Anyone reversing course on taxes would lose me entirely.

new_opinion.reasoning: After everything I've heard, I'm leaning greenway. Taxes is still my number one issue and that's what I'm voting on.

conversation.topic: Bus 7's suspension surviving another winter of Route 9

events.text: Honestly, taxes is what keeps me up at night. I want a real plan before decision day, not another speech.
Between you and me, taxes has only gotten worse this year. That's the lens I'm bringing to this decision.
I keep coming back to rent. Everyone around here feels it, and none of the options fully convince me yet.

events.summary: Hearing another perspective on rent gave me something to chew on.; I came away thinking harder about taxes.

events.headline: Engineering Report Doubles Main Street Bridge Repair Estimate to $6.8M
events.description: The structural review the Select Board commissioned from Hartwell & Sloane Engineering finds pier scour and deck delamination on the 1936 Main Street Bridge, doubling the repair estimate from $3.2M to $6.8M. The weight limit drops from 15 tons to 8 tons effective immediately: Engine 2 and the school buses must now detour the Route 9 loop, adding roughly eleven minutes to any cross-river run.

events.headline: Engineering Report Doubles Main Street Bridge Repair Estimate to $6.8M
events.description: The structural review the Select Board commissioned from Hartwell & Sloane Engineering finds pier scour and deck delamination on the 1936 Main Street Bridge, doubling the repair estimate from $3.2M to $6.8M. The weight limit drops from 15 tons to 8 tons effective immediately: Engine 2 and the school buses must now detour the Route 9 loop, adding roughly eleven minutes to any cross-river run.

events.text: Re: Engineering Report Doubles Main Street Bridge Repa... - I read this twice. Anything touching jobs touches my household, so I can't just shrug it off.

events.headline: Anonymous Donor Pledges $2M Match for the Riverwalk Greenway
events.description: A letter delivered through the Millbrook Community Trust pledges a dollar-for-dollar match, up to $2 million, for Riverwalk Greenway construction — contingent on the town committing the surplus at the November 3 meeting. The donor is unnamed. The Trust says the match would extend the trail across the river to Community Fields. The letter was read aloud twice on the Wheelhouse Diner porch before lunch.

events.headline: State Comptroller Warns Towns: Pension Fund Returns 'Overstated'
events.description: A state comptroller bulletin lowers the assumed rate of return on municipal pension funds from 6.9% to 6.0%. Recalculated overnight, Millbrook's unfunded pension liability grows from $4.1M to $5.9M, and the finance committee schedules an emergency session. Older residents remember 1998, when a mid-year aid cut and a failed revaluation left the town eleven days from missing payroll.

events.headline: State Comptroller Warns Towns: Pension Fund Returns 'Overstated'

events.text: Between you and me, traffic has only gotten worse this year. That's the lens I'm bringing to this decision.
